text:
In recent years, Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) have been becoming more significant in the civil and public safety areas, where many high-risk activities such as search and rescue, traffic monitoring and environmental surveillance require strong detection and assessment to protect the lives and property of human beings. Although multi-UAV system can obviously realize more work with higher working efficiency and economy than a single UAV, stable and context-aware networking remains one of the biggest challenges to prevent aerial systems from performing unsafe missions. In fact, even though MANETs and VANETs represent relatively stable network structure for mobile ground terminals, aerial vehicles in drone swarms behave differently in highly dynamic airspace where their network topologies keep on changing frequently while communication links often break up due to dynamic distances caused by rapid movement of flying vehicles.

The purpose of this paper is to address the challenges that should be taken into consideration in order to provide reliable multi-UAV communication systems. Also, the use of Software Defined Networking (SDN) technologies in order to improve the overall system structure and to provide increased level of system security is discussed. New routing protocols and techniques are required that would efficiently manage the UAVs limited transmission energy resources and that will efficiently handle the network fragmentation using delay-tolerant networking concepts. Also handover mechanisms should be introduced in order to handle the abrupt links’ breakages due to UAVs failure as well as efficient power management techniques need to be investigated across multiple layers in order to enhance the lifespan of the system. In this paper an overall review is performed about the challenges that must be addressed by air communication networks technologies, by combining currently existing research works, while also highlighting the open issues that have not yet been studied and therefore providing also a vision of future Airborne Communication Networks.
